title: Numerical security analysis for practical quantum key distribution
authors: Álvaro Navarrete, Guillermo Currás-Lorenzo, Margarida Pereira, Marcos Curty
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) promises information-theoretic security based on quantum mechanics and idealized device models. Practical implementations, however, deviate from these models due to unavoidable device imperfections, and existing security proofs fall short of capturing the complexity of real-world systems. Here we introduce a versatile numerical finite-key security framework valid against general coherent attacks and applicable to a broad class of practical QKD setups. It accommodates most relevant imperfections at both transmitter and receiver, including non-independent-and-identically-distributed (non-IID) signals arising in high-speed QKD systems due to the limited bandwidth of optical modulators, while requiring only partial characterization of the apparatuses. We demonstrate the power of our framework by proving the security of a realistic decoy-state QKD implementation with laser sources, providing a practical route towards rigorous security certification of real-world QKD setups.
summary: This paper introduces a numerical framework to certify the security of practical QKD systems against complex, real-world imperfections like device flaws and correlated signals. By using semidefinite programming, the authors achieve tighter security bounds than existing analytical methods. This allows for more robust and reliable implementation of quantum key distribution in practical fiber-optic networks.
main_problem: Bridging the gap between idealized security proofs and practical QKD implementations by addressing device imperfections and non-IID signal correlations.
main_result: A versatile numerical finite-key security framework that provides tight, probabilistic upper bounds on phase error rates against general coherent attacks, even in the presence of pulse correlations and detector mismatches.
method: The authors utilize Semidefinite Programming (SDP) to derive operator inequalities and a Virtual Estimation Protocol (VEP) to bound errors using observable statistics.
model_or_system: Practical QKD setups, specifically BB84-type prepare-and-measure protocols and decoy-state BB84, including models for state-preparation flaws, detector efficiency mismatch, and correlated photon pulses.
key_observables: Phase-error rate (e_ph), secret-key rate (l_key), number of sifted-key bits (N_key), and error statistics from test rounds.
important_parameters: Correlation length (L), security parameters (epsilon), detection efficiency (eta_det), dark-count probability (p_d), and number of protocol rounds (N).
main_assumptions: Assumes a fidelity constraint between prepared and reference states and assumes Bob's measurements satisfy the Basis-Independent Detection Efficiency (BIDE) condition.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows optimized secret-key rates for single-photon BB84 under various imperfections; Figure 2 shows performance for decoy-state BB84 with complex correlations; Figure 3 compares the proposed numerical proof against state-of-the-art analytical proofs.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the theory-practice gap, presents a numerical framework using SDP and VEP, provides mathematical proofs for error bounding and security equivalence, and demonstrates the framework's power through simulations of BB84 and decoy-state protocols.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous way to certify the security of real-world quantum communication hardware, which is essential for the deployment of scalable quantum networks and the protection of quantum information against coherent attacks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Optimized secret-key rate of a single-photon BB84 scheme in the presence of multiple device imperfections. For all lines, we consider SPFs with ∆θ = 0.063 [48, 49], and we set the number of protocol rounds to N = 1010 (except for the black line, which assumes the ideal asymptotic case in the absence of any imperfection). Besides, we consider that the quantum states emitted by Alice are partially uncharacterized and correlated, which is modeled via the parameter ε and the correlation length L. For the receiver, we fix the detector tolerances to the same value, i.e., ∆η = ∆dc = ∆D.

Figure 2. Optimized secret-key rate of a decoy-state BB84 QKD scheme with a laser source and three intensity settings in the presence of multiple device imperfections. For all lines, we consider SPFs with ∆θ = 0.063 [48, 49], and we set the number of protocol rounds to N = 1011 (except for the black line, which considers the ideal asymptotic case in the absence of any imperfection). Besides, we consider that the quantum states emitted by Alice are partially characterized and corre- lated, which is modeled via the parameter ε and the correla- tion length L. For the receiver, we fix the detector tolerances to the same value, i.e., ∆η = ∆dc = ∆D.

Figure 3. Optimized secret-key rate of a single-photon BB84 scheme obtained with our numerical security proof (blue dotted lines) and the analytical security proof of [20] (gray solid lines). We consider SPFs with parameter ∆θ = 0.063 [48, 49], a pessimistic dark-count probability pd = 10−6, and a detection efficiency of Bob’s detectors equal to ηdet = 0.73 [50]. In Fig. 3a, we fix N = 1010 and run the simulations for different values of ε, while in Fig. 3b we fix ε = 10−5 and run the simulations for different values of N.

Figure 4. Secret-key rate of a coherent-light-based MDI QKD scheme [23] in the presence of device imperfections. The green (purple) lines illustrate the scenarios ε = {10−3, 10−5, 10−7, 0} and N →∞(N = 1010). The dotted (solid) lines correspond to the results provided by our numerical security proof (the analytical security proof of [20]). In the simulations, we consider a lossy but noiseless channel model (see e.g. [20]), in which the detectors have a detection efficiency ηdet = 0.73 and a dark-count probability pd = 10−8 [50]. The security parameters are set to ϵsec = ϵEV = 10−10.
